enrichment.short_description: Open-source AI agent platform for building chatbots and automating customer support across web chat, WhatsApp, and other channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tiledesk started as an open-source live chat platform and has evolved into what it now calls an AI agent platform, positioned as an open-source alternative to tools like Voiceflow, Intercom, and Zendesk. The core server, dashboard, chatbot engine, and web widget are published on GitHub under open-source licenses (a mix of MIT and AGPLv3 depending on the repository) and can be self-hosted using Docker Compose or a Helm chart for Kubernetes.

Beyond self-hosting, Tiledesk also runs a hosted cloud service with paid subscription plans. The platform's visual Design Studio lets teams build automated conversation flows without writing code, route conversations between AI agents and human staff, and connect a knowledge base for retrieval-augmented (RAG) responses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Tiledesk suits support and operations teams that want to automate parts of customer service — e-commerce stores handling order questions, SaaS companies fielding repetitive support tickets, or agencies building white-labeled chat automation for clients. Its self-hosting option also appeals to developers and organizations with data residency requirements who want to connect their own LLMs instead of sending data to third-party AI providers.

features.title: Visual Design Studio
features.description: A no-code flow builder for designing chatbot logic, decision trees, and automated conversation paths.

features.title: Multi-channel deployment
features.description: Deploy the same bot or live chat widget across a website, WhatsApp Business, Facebook Messenger, SMS, and email.

features.title: Knowledge base & RAG
features.description: Upload documents to a knowledge base that AI agents search using hybrid full-text and semantic search to ground their answers.

features.title: Human handoff
features.description: Conversations can be escalated from an AI agent to a live human operator without losing context.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Run Tiledesk on your own infrastructure via Docker Compose or Kubernetes, or use the managed cloud service.

features.title: Open API and integrations
features.description: REST APIs and integrations with tools like Shopify, Google Calendar, and Outlook for connecting Tiledesk to existing workflows.

pros: Core platform is open source and can be fully self-hosted at no license cost
Supports multiple channels (web, WhatsApp, Messenger, SMS, email) from one platform
No-code flow builder makes bot design accessible to non-developers
Can connect to self-hosted or open LLMs for organizations that need to keep data on-premises
cons: Cloud pricing is steep for small teams — paid plans start around €124-149/month
Self-hosting requires Docker/Kubernetes knowledge and ongoing maintenance
The product has shifted heavily toward AI agent automation, so teams wanting a simple live-chat widget may find the interface more complex than earlier versions

faqs.question: Is Tiledesk free to use?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted version is free and open source under licenses published on GitHub. The managed cloud service is paid, with plans starting after a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Tiledesk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tiledesk provides Docker Compose files and a Helm chart so you can install the server, dashboard, and chatbot engine on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What channels does Tiledesk support?
faqs.answer: Web chat widget, WhatsApp Business, Facebook Messenger, SMS, and email, all managed from the same dashboard.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to build a bot?
faqs.answer: No. The Design Studio is a visual, no-code flow builder, though custom logic can also be extended with code where needed.

use_cases.title: E-commerce order support
use_cases.description: Automate answers to shipping, returns, and order-status questions while escalating complex cases to a human agent.

use_cases.title: On-premises AI support agent
use_cases.description: Self-host Tiledesk and connect it to a local or open LLM to keep customer data off third-party AI APIs.

use_cases.title: WhatsApp business automation
use_cases.description: Route and automate customer conversations coming in through WhatsApp Business alongside the website widget.

features: Unlimited users, agents, tokens and conversations

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tiledesk is an open-source AI agent platform for building chatbots and automating customer support, self-hostable via Docker or available as a paid cloud service.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source peer-to-peer protocol for encrypted messaging, voice/video calls, and file transfer with no central servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tox is an open-source protocol for encrypted instant messaging, voice and video calls, screen sharing, and file transfer, built as a peer-to-peer alternative to centralized services like Skype. Development began in mid-2013, prompted by concerns over Skype's ties to government surveillance programs that surfaced around the Edward Snowden leaks. Rather than a single application, Tox is a core library (toxcore) that independent client applications build on, the most widely used being qTox.
Because there's no central server relaying messages, Tox connections happen directly between users' devices over a distributed network, encrypted end-to-end using the NaCl cryptography library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Tox is aimed at privacy-focused users who want end-to-end encrypted communication without trusting a company's servers with their metadata. Because there's no central server to seize, subpoena, or shut down, it appeals to activists, journalists, and technically inclined users comfortable installing a standalone client rather than using a mainstream messaging app.

features.title: Peer-to-peer architecture
features.description: Messages and calls route directly between users' devices over a distributed network, with no central server storing data.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: All communications are encrypted using the NaCl cryptography library; encryption cannot be disabled.

features.title: Voice and video calls
features.description: Supports one-to-one and group audio/video calls plus screen sharing.

features.title: File transfer
features.description: Send files directly between contacts without size limits imposed by a server.

features.title: Multiple independent clients
features.description: Several client apps, including qTox, are built on the shared toxcore library, giving users a choice of interface.

pros: No central server means no company can hand over your message history
Completely free with no ads, subscriptions, or accounts tied to a phone number
Supports calls, video, screen sharing, and file transfer, not just text
Fully open-source protocol and clients
cons: As a P2P system, both contacts generally need to be reachable for messages and calls to go through reliably
Smaller user base than mainstream messengers makes it harder to get contacts onto the network
No single official mobile app; support on Android and iOS depends on third-party clients of varying maturity
Less polished UX than commercial messaging apps

faqs.question: What is Tox?
faqs.answer: Tox is an open-source, peer-to-peer protocol for encrypted messaging, voice/video calls, and file transfer, developed as a decentralized alternative to Skype.

faqs.question: Do I need to run a server to use Tox?
faqs.answer: No. Tox is peer-to-peer — each client connects directly to other users' devices over a distributed network rather than through a central server.

faqs.question: Which app do I install to use Tox?
faqs.answer: You install a Tox client, such as qTox, which implements the toxcore library and provides the messaging interface.

faqs.question: Is Tox free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tox and its clients are free, open-source software with no cost or advertising.

use_cases.title: Private one-to-one messaging
use_cases.description: Communicate with contacts without routing messages through a company-operated server.

use_cases.title: Encrypted voice/video calls
use_cases.description: Make calls and screen-share directly with contacts using end-to-end encryption.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tox is a free, open-source peer-to-peer protocol for encrypted messaging, calls, and file transfer with no central server, used via clients like qTox.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted Matrix homeserver built for high performance, positioned as the official successor to Conduwuit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tuwunel is a Matrix homeserver — the server software that powers decentralized, federated chat on the Matrix protocol used by clients like Element. It's built to be fast and resource-efficient, and it describes itself as the official successor to Conduwuit, continuing that project's development after Conduwuit reached a stable, production-ready state. Migrating from Conduwuit to Tuwunel is designed to be a straightforward binary swap since Tuwunel maintains configuration and database compatibility.

Tuwunel targets production, at-scale deployments: its documentation cites usage serving government infrastructure and notes independent benchmark results showing it outperforming Synapse, the original reference Matrix server, in several test scenarios.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Tuwunel is for system administrators and organizations who want to self-host a Matrix homeserver for team or community chat instead of relying on public Matrix.org infrastructure, particularly those who have outgrown Synapse's resource footprint or need enterprise authentication (OAuth 2.0, OIDC, LDAP, JWT) and Matrix RTC voice/video support.

features.title: Matrix protocol compliance
features.description: Implements the Matrix client-server and federation APIs, working with standard Matrix clients such as Element.

features.title: High throughput, low resource use
features.description: Built for efficient message handling with lower CPU and memory usage compared to Synapse, the original Matrix homeserver implementation.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: Supports OAuth 2.0, OIDC, LDAP, and JWT for integrating with existing identity systems.

features.title: Matrix RTC support
features.description: Supports Matrix's real-time communication extensions for voice and video calls.

features.title: Drop-in Conduwuit migration
features.description: Existing Conduwuit deployments can move to Tuwunel by swapping the binary, without a separate data migration step.

pros: Free, open-source, self-hosted Matrix homeserver
Significantly lighter on server resources than Synapse in independent benchmarks
Federates with the wider Matrix network, so users aren't locked into a single server
Active development backed by real production deployments
cons: Requires comfort administering server infrastructure — there's no managed hosting offered by the project itself
As a newer project (successor to Conduwuit), it has a shorter track record than Synapse
Full Matrix specification coverage is still catching up in some areas per its own test-suite reporting

faqs.question: Is Tuwunel free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tuwunel is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: How is Tuwunel different from Synapse?
faqs.answer: Tuwunel is a from-scratch, performance-focused Matrix homeserver implementation, whereas Synapse is the original Matrix.org reference server. Tuwunel targets lower resource usage and higher throughput.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from Conduwuit to Tuwunel?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project describes it as a binary swap — you update to Tuwunel and point your service at the new executable without a separate data migration.

faqs.question: Do I need a Matrix client to use Tuwunel?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tuwunel is server software; you connect to it using a standard Matrix client such as Element.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team or community chat
use_cases.description: Run your own Matrix server for internal team communication instead of depending on a third-party chat provider.

use_cases.title: Federated messaging at scale
use_cases.description: Serve large user bases with lower server resource requirements than Synapse.
use_cases.audience: Organizations replacing or scaling past Synapse

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tuwunel is a free, open-source, self-hosted Matrix homeserver built for performance, positioned as the official successor to Conduwuit.

enrichment.short_description: Source-available, self-hostable chatbot and conversational form builder with a visual, no-code editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Typebot is a visual builder for chatbots and conversational forms, letting teams design multi-step conversation flows using more than 45 drag-and-drop blocks (text, images, video, buttons, date pickers, payment inputs, and more) without writing code. Bots can be embedded on websites, deployed as a chat bubble or popup, connected to WhatsApp, or triggered over HTTP requests, and results feed into built-in analytics tracking drop-off and completion rates.

Founded by developer Baptiste Arnaud in 2020, Typebot was long distributed as open-source software under AGPLv3. In 2026 the project moved its source code to the Functional Source License, a 'Fair Source' license: the code remains publicly viewable and self-hostable, but competitors are restricted from using it to build a competing product, and it automatically converts to the permissive Apache 2.0 license two years after each release.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Typebot suits marketing, support, and sales teams building lead-capture forms, product quizzes, or support chatbots, as well as developers who want to self-host a chatbot builder instead of paying for a closed SaaS platform.

features.title: Visual flow builder
features.description: Drag-and-drop editor with 45+ input and logic blocks for building multi-step chatbots and forms.

features.title: Multi-channel deployment
features.description: Embed on a website, use a chat bubble or popup, deploy to WhatsApp, or call flows via HTTP request.

features.title: Native integrations
features.description: Connects to OpenAI, Google Sheets, Zapier, Google Analytics, and Meta Pixel out of the box.

features.title: Analytics
features.description: Tracks completion and drop-off rates per step, with CSV export of collected results.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: The source is publicly available and can be deployed on your own infrastructure instead of using the hosted cloud service.

features.title: Custom theming and code
features.description: Advanced theming plus custom JavaScript/CSS for tailoring the bot's appearance and behavior.

pros: Generous free plan (200 chats/month, unlimited bots) with no credit card required
Self-hostable for teams that want full control over data and hosting
Large block library covers most common form/chatbot needs without coding
AI-provider agnostic — works with OpenAI and other providers rather than locking you into one
cons: As of 2026 the source code is under the Functional Source License, not a traditional OSI-approved open-source license — commercial competitors are restricted from using it
Paid cloud plans (Starter at $39/month, Pro at $89/month) are priced for small businesses, not individual hobbyists
Self-hosting requires running and maintaining your own infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Typebot free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there's a free plan covering 200 chats/month and unlimited typebots. Paid plans start at $39/month for higher chat volume and extra features.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Typebot?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is publicly available and documented for self-hosting on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Typebot open source?
faqs.answer: Typebot was historically AGPLv3-licensed; since 2026 its source is published under the Functional Source License (Fair Source), which keeps the code publicly viewable and self-hostable but restricts building a competing commercial product with it.

faqs.question: Does Typebot support AI chatbots?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with OpenAI and other LLM providers so you can add AI-generated responses inside a flow.

use_cases.title: Lead-capture conversational forms
use_cases.description: Replace static web forms with an interactive, multi-step flow that improves completion rates.

use_cases.title: WhatsApp support automation
use_cases.description: Deploy a Typebot flow to WhatsApp to answer common questions or qualify leads.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted internal tools
use_cases.description: Run Typebot on internal infrastructure to build data-collection forms without sending data to a third party.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Typebot is a self-hostable, no-code chatbot and conversational form builder with a visual flow editor and a free plan for up to 200 chats/month.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source email alias service that forwards anonymized addresses to your real inbox, self-hostable or usable as addy.io.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AnonAddy is an open-source email forwarding and alias service: instead of giving out your real email address, you generate unique aliases (per website or fully random) that forward incoming mail to your actual inbox, and you can reply from an alias without revealing your real address to the recipient. The project rebranded to addy.io in 2023, though the underlying open-source project and GitHub organization are still commonly referred to by the AnonAddy name.

The software is built on Laravel/PHP with Postfix for mail handling, and it's fully open source, so it can be self-hosted on your own server using the official Docker image instead of using the hosted addy.io service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious individuals who want to stop giving their real email address to every website they sign up for, reduce spam and the impact of data breaches, and — for technical users — run their own alias infrastructure instead of trusting a third party.

features.title: Unlimited alias creation
features.description: Generate unique email aliases per website, either based on your username or fully random, that forward to your real inbox.

features.title: Anonymous replies
features.description: Reply to forwarded messages from an alias without exposing your real email address to the recipient.

features.title: Custom domains
features.description: Use your own domain name for aliases instead of the shared addy.io domain.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Extensions for Firefox, Chrome, Edge, Safari, and other Chromium browsers to generate aliases while browsing.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps for managing aliases on the go.

features.title: GPG/OpenPGP encryption
features.description: Optionally encrypt forwarded email content so only you can read it, even from the mail server.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: REST API for automating alias creation and account management.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Fully open-source codebase with an official Docker image for running your own instance.

pros: Free tier available with no cost to start protecting your real email address
Fully open source with an official self-hosting path if you don't want to trust the hosted service
Browser extensions and mobile apps make alias creation convenient
Optional PGP encryption adds an extra layer of privacy for forwarded mail
cons: Free plan is limited to 10 shared-domain aliases, 1 recipient, and 10MB bandwidth
Self-hosting requires managing your own mail server (Postfix), database, and Redis instance
Anonymous replies from an alias require a paid plan

faqs.question: Is AnonAddy the same as addy.io?
faqs.answer: Yes. The service rebranded to addy.io in 2023; the open-source project is still often referred to by its original AnonAddy name.

faqs.question: Is AnonAddy free?
faqs.answer: There's a free plan with 10 shared-domain aliases and 1 recipient. Paid plans (Lite and Pro) add custom domains, more recipients, and anonymous replies.

faqs.question: Can I self-host AnonAddy?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is open and there's an official Docker image, though you'll need to run your own Postfix mail server, database, and Redis instance.

faqs.question: Can I reply from an alias without revealing my real address?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is supported as a paid feature — replies sent from an alias appear to come from that alias, not your real inbox.

use_cases.title: Reducing signup spam
use_cases.description: Use a unique alias for every online account so you can identify and disable the address if it starts receiving spam or appears in a breach.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted alias infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run your own alias/forwarding service on a custom domain instead of trusting a third-party provider with your mail metadata.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AnonAddy (now addy.io) is an open-source email alias service that forwards anonymized addresses to your inbox, free to start or self-hostable.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted PHP webmail platform for running your own email service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: b1gMail is a PHP-based webmail and mail server management platform that lets you run your own email service for a domain — for personal use, a club or organization, or a business — without needing a dedicated root server, since it runs on standard PHP web hosting. It provides a modern web interface with drag-and-drop message handling, along with bundled tools like an address book, calendar, task manager, notes, and basic cloud storage.

Originally distributed as commercial/freeware software by its creator, Patrick Schlangen, b1gMail changed its license to GPL starting with version 7.4.1, removing its proprietary components and making the full platform free and open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Website owners, clubs and associations, and small organizations that want to offer branded email addresses on their own domain without paying for a hosted business email plan, and who are comfortable running PHP-based web software.

features.title: Web-based mail client
features.description: HTML5 webmail interface with drag-and-drop message organization and intelligent folder/filter rules.

features.title: Bundled productivity tools
features.description: Includes an address book, calendar, task manager, notes, and basic cloud storage alongside email.

features.title: Spam and virus protection
features.description: DNSBL-based filtering and Bayesian spam analysis, with optional ClamAV integration for virus scanning.

features.title: Plugin system
features.description: Extensible via plugins for adding custom functionality.

features.title: Runs on shared PHP hosting
features.description: Doesn't require a dedicated root server — deploys on standard PHP-capable web hosting.

pros: Fully free and open source (GPL) since version 7.4.1
Runs on ordinary PHP web hosting instead of requiring a dedicated mail server
Bundles calendar, contacts, and task tools alongside email in one webmail interface
Built-in spam and virus filtering
cons: Interface and community documentation historically skew toward German-speaking users
Managing spam and deliverability on a shared-hosting mail setup takes more effort than a managed mail provider
Smaller plugin and extension ecosystem than larger mail platforms

faqs.question: Is b1gMail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, since version 7.4.1 b1gMail is licensed under the GPL and its proprietary components were removed, making it free and open source.

faqs.question: Do I need a dedicated server to run b1gMail?
faqs.answer: No, it's designed to run on standard PHP web hosting rather than requiring a dedicated root server.

faqs.question: Does b1gMail include anything besides email?
faqs.answer: Yes, it bundles an address book, calendar, task manager, notes, and basic cloud storage alongside the webmail client.

use_cases.title: Branded email for a club or small business
use_cases.description: Offer @yourdomain email addresses to members or staff without paying for a hosted business email plan.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted webmail on shared hosting
use_cases.description: Run a full webmail platform on standard PHP hosting instead of provisioning a dedicated mail server.
use_cases.audience: Website owners with existing PHP hosting

enrichment.seo_meta_description: b1gMail is a free, open-source PHP webmail platform for self-hosting your own branded email service on standard web hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source collection of Ansible roles and playbooks for automating Debian/Ubuntu infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DebOps is a collection of Ansible roles and playbooks for bootstrapping and managing IT infrastructure built on Debian or Ubuntu, from a single host to a full data center. Started by Maciej Delmanowski in October 2013, it packages together dozens of integrated Ansible roles covering things like TLS certificate management (with Let's Encrypt), GitLab-based Git hosting, LibreNMS network monitoring, and Nextcloud/ownCloud file sharing, so administrators don't have to write and maintain their own Ansible automation from scratch.
The project can target physical machines, virtual machines, or containers (LXC/Docker), and its roles are also published individually on Ansible Galaxy for use outside the full DebOps playbook set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: System administrators and DevOps engineers managing Debian/Ubuntu server fleets who want a maintained, integrated set of Ansible automation instead of building every role themselves.

features.title: Integrated Ansible roles
features.description: Dozens of roles for common infrastructure services, designed to work together rather than as isolated one-off scripts.

features.title: TLS/certificate management
features.description: Automates X.509 certificate issuance and renewal, including Let's Encrypt integration.

features.title: Self-hosted service playbooks
features.description: Includes playbooks for services like GitLab, LibreNMS, and Nextcloud/ownCloud.

features.title: Multi-target deployment
features.description: Works against physical servers, VMs, or LXC/Docker containers.

features.title: Published on Ansible Galaxy
features.description: Individual roles can be used independently of the full DebOps playbook collection.

pros: Free and open source with an active project history dating back to 2013
Covers a broad set of common infrastructure needs out of the box, reducing custom Ansible work
Designed for Debian/Ubuntu specifically, so roles are tuned to those distributions rather than generic
Roles are reusable individually via Ansible Galaxy
cons: Debian/Ubuntu-specific — not designed for other Linux distributions
Requires existing familiarity with Ansible to configure and extend
Documentation and community are smaller than mainstream configuration-management tools

faqs.question: What is DebOps?
faqs.answer: DebOps is a free, open-source set of Ansible roles and playbooks for automating the setup and management of Debian/Ubuntu infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does DebOps work with distributions other than Debian/Ubuntu?
faqs.answer: It's specifically designed for Debian and Ubuntu-based systems.

faqs.question: Can I use individual DebOps roles without the whole project?
faqs.answer: Yes, roles are published on Ansible Galaxy and can be used independently of the full DebOps playbook set.

use_cases.title: Standardized server provisioning
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent, tested set of Ansible roles across a fleet of Debian/Ubuntu servers.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted infrastructure automation
use_cases.description: Automate deployment of self-hosted services like GitLab, Nextcloud, and monitoring tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DebOps is a free, open-source collection of Ansible roles and playbooks for automating Debian and Ubuntu server infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source Docker image bundling a full mail server (SMTP, IMAP, spam/virus filtering) configured via files, not a database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: docker-mailserver (DMS) is a production-ready, containerized mail server bundling SMTP, IMAP, optional LDAP authentication, anti-spam, and anti-virus into a single Docker image. Rather than requiring a SQL database to manage accounts and settings, it's configured through plain configuration files, which the project positions as simpler to version-control and reason about than database-backed mail server stacks.

The project is MIT-licensed and maintained on GitHub by the Docker Mailserver organization, with images published to Docker Hub and GitHub Container Registry. A bundled setup.sh script helps with day-to-day administration tasks like adding accounts and managing DKIM keys.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters and system administrators who want to run their own email server in Docker without configuring Postfix, Dovecot, and spam/antivirus tooling by hand, from hobbyists running a personal domain to admins managing mail for an organization.

features.title: Full mail stack in one container
features.description: Bundles SMTP, IMAP, and optional LDAP authentication in a single Docker image.

features.title: Anti-spam and anti-virus
features.description: Includes spam filtering and virus scanning out of the box.

features.title: Configuration-file based
features.description: Accounts and settings are managed through plain config files rather than a SQL database, making setup easier to version-control.

features.title: setup.sh administration script
features.description: A bundled CLI script for common tasks like adding mail accounts and managing DKIM keys.

features.title: Published to Docker Hub and GHCR
features.description: Prebuilt images are available from both Docker Hub (mailserver/docker-mailserver) and GitHub Container Registry.

pros: Free, open-source (MIT license), no vendor lock-in
Avoids the complexity of a database-backed mail account system
Actively maintained with regular image updates
Bundles anti-spam and anti-virus rather than requiring separate setup
cons: Running your own mail server still requires correctly managing DNS records (SPF, DKIM, DMARC, reverse DNS) for deliverability
No graphical admin UI — administration is via config files and the setup.sh CLI
You're responsible for your own backups, updates, and abuse/spam monitoring

faqs.question: Is docker-mailserver free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open-source software under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does it use a database?
faqs.answer: No, it's intentionally configured via flat configuration files instead of a SQL database.

faqs.question: Does it include spam and virus filtering?
faqs.answer: Yes, anti-spam and anti-virus scanning are included as part of the bundled mail stack.

faqs.question: Where do I get the image?
faqs.answer: It's published on Docker Hub as mailserver/docker-mailserver and also on GitHub Container Registry.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted personal or business email
use_cases.description: Run your own mail server for a domain in a single Docker container instead of using a hosted mail provider.

use_cases.title: Reproducible mail server configuration
use_cases.description: Manage mail server settings as version-controlled config files rather than database records.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: docker-mailserver is a free, open-source Docker image that bundles SMTP, IMAP, anti-spam, and anti-virus into a config-file-based mail server.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, open-source SMTP mail server written in Go, configured with a single JSON file plus an optional web interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dovel is a minimalist, self-hosted SMTP server for sending and receiving email, written in Go and licensed under LGPL-3.0. It's configured through a single JSON file, supports handling mail for multiple domains, and includes DKIM signing and PGP support via the Web Key Directory (WKD) standard. An optional lightweight web interface is available for browsing received email in a browser instead of relying solely on a mail client.

The project describes itself as being in early, active development and uses only a few megabytes of RAM, positioning it as a much smaller alternative to full mail-server stacks like docker-mailserver or Mailcow for people who want a simple, low-resource mail server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and self-hosters who want a small, easy-to-configure mail server for a personal domain or a small number of mailboxes, and who are comfortable running early-stage software with a smaller feature set than mature mail server projects.

features.title: Single-file JSON configuration
features.description: The entire server is configured through one JSON file rather than a multi-service stack.

features.title: Multi-domain support
features.description: Can handle mail for more than one domain from a single instance.

features.title: DKIM signing
features.description: Supports DKIM by pointing the config at a key file.

features.title: PGP via WKD
features.description: Supports PGP encryption discovery using the Web Key Directory standard.

features.title: Optional web interface
features.description: A lightweight built-in web UI for browsing received email without a separate mail client.

pros: Very lightweight — uses only a few MBs of RAM
Simple, single-file configuration instead of a multi-component mail stack
Free and open source under LGPL-3.0
Built-in DKIM and PGP/WKD support
cons: Explicitly described by its author as early-stage and actively in development, so features and stability are still evolving
Much smaller community and track record than established mail servers like docker-mailserver or Mailcow
Documentation is minimal compared to mature self-hosted mail projects

faqs.question: Is Dovel free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dovel is free, open-source software under the LGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: What language is Dovel written in?
faqs.answer: Go.

faqs.question: Does Dovel have a web interface?
faqs.answer: Yes, an optional lightweight web interface is included for browsing your email in a browser.

faqs.question: Is Dovel production-ready?
faqs.answer: The project describes itself as being in early, active development, so it may suit hobby or small personal deployments more than mission-critical production mail.

use_cases.title: Lightweight personal mail server
use_cases.description: Run a small, low-resource mail server for a personal domain without deploying a full mail-server stack.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovel is a lightweight, open-source SMTP mail server written in Go, configured with a single JSON file and an optional web interface.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source disposable/anonymous email software; its public hosted instance at inboxen.org has shut down, but the code remains self-hostable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Inboxen is open-source software for generating disposable, anonymous email inboxes that forward to a real address, aimed at letting users sign up for websites without exposing a permanent email identity. The project's public hosted service at inboxen.org shut down in 2026, with the team taking the website offline and deleting hosted user data. The source code, however, remains published (now hosted on Codeberg after moving from GitHub) and continues to accept community pull requests, meaning the software can still be self-hosted even though the original public instance is gone.
Because official maintenance has slowed to a reduced cadence since the shutdown, Inboxen is best understood today as a self-hosted project you run yourself rather than a service you can sign up for.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Technical users who want to run their own disposable-email infrastructure and are comfortable setting up the required stack (Python, PostgreSQL, Node.js) rather than relying on a hosted disposable-email provider.

features.title: Disposable inbox generation
features.description: Creates anonymous, disposable email addresses that forward to a real mailbox.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Source available on Codeberg with setup instructions for running your own instance using Python, PostgreSQL, and Node.js.

features.title: Open development
features.description: The maintainer continues to accept community pull requests despite the hosted service being retired.

pros: Source code remains open and self-hostable even after the public service shut down
Free, no-cost software for anyone willing to run it themselves
Still accepting community contributions and pull requests
cons: The public hosted instance at inboxen.org has permanently shut down, so there's no longer a ready-to-use hosted option
Official maintenance is explicitly reduced since the shutdown
Self-hosting requires setting up a multi-part stack (Python, PostgreSQL, Node.js) yourself

faqs.question: Is Inboxen still available as a hosted service?
faqs.answer: No. The public instance at inboxen.org shut down in 2026 and its hosted user data was deleted. The software itself remains available to self-host.

faqs.question: Can I still use Inboxen?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only by self-hosting it yourself from the source code published on Codeberg — there is no longer a public sign-up service.

faqs.question: Is the project still maintained?
faqs.answer: The maintainer has stated the repository receives fewer updates since the shutdown but is still accepting pull requests.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted disposable email
use_cases.description: Run your own instance to generate disposable inboxes for yourself or a small group instead of using a third-party disposable-email service.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Inboxen is open-source disposable email software. Its public hosted instance has shut down, but the code remains available to self-host.
